title: Death marks the spot: The hunt for Forrest Fenn's treasure

passage.title: Death marks the spot: The hunt for Forrest Fenn’s treasure
passage.text:
As I have gone alone in there  
And with my treasures bold,  
I can keep my secret where,  
And hint of riches new and old.  
Begin it where warm waters halt  
And take it in the canyon down,  
Not far, but too far to walk.  
Put in below the home of Brown.  
From there it’s no place for the [word],  
The end is drawing ever [word];  
There’ll be no paddle up your creek,  
Just heavy loads and water high.  
If you’ve been wise and found the blaze,  
Look quickly down, your quest to [word],  
But [word] [word] with marvel gaze,  
Just take the chest and go in peace.  
So why is it that I must go  
And leave my [word] for all to seek?  
The answer I already know  
I’ve done it tired, and now I’m weak.  
So hear me all and listen good,  
Your effort will be worth the cold.  
If you are brave and in the wood  
I give you [word] to the gold.  

–By Forrest Fenn, in *The Thrill of the Chase*


1. Over the course of 13 years, Forrest Fenn’s poem would inspire lawsuits, crimes, and fatal journeys into the wilderness. But how could such an [word] piece of art be the source of so much controversy and death? The answer to that question begins with the poem's author.

###The man behind the hunt

2. An [word] art dealer with a love of the outdoors, Fenn was inspired by a cancer diagnosis to establish a [word], something that would inspire others to explore the wilderness and seek adventure. He began work on a manuscript.

3. Fenn recovered from cancer, and in 2010 he released his memoir, stating that it contained clues to the location of a treasure chest brimming with gold, jewels, and rare coins. He noted that the poem above was a crucial component of the hunt, claiming that it held nine clues to the treasure’s location. Searchers were directed to seek the treasure “somewhere in the mountains north of Santa Fe.”

###The race begins

4. Word about Fenn’s treasure hunt was slow to spread. However, after Fenn discussed his book in a 2013 television interview, stores sold out of the memoir and seekers flooded the Rocky Mountains. In the pivotal 2013 interview, he emphasized his hope that the treasure hunt would get families outside together, encouraging parents to “[g]et your kids out in the countryside, take them fishing and get them away from their little hand-held machines."

5. It’s estimated that over 350,000 individuals attempted to find the treasure. Some gave up their jobs, while others engaged in criminal activity—grave-digging, burglarizing Fenn’s house, and trespassing, among others—in the hopes of furthering their chances. And five people paid for their attempts with their lives, dying in accidents out in the wilderness. At one point, the governor of New Mexico [word] Fenn to call off the search, to prevent further loss of life. In response, Fenn reminded treasure hunters that he had hidden the treasure as an 80-year-old, so reaching it wouldn’t require climbing, swimming, or any dangerous activities.

###An unsatisfying ending

6. In June 2020, Fenn announced that the treasure had been found. However, he refused to disclose the name of the finder, who wished to remain anonymous, nor the location, which he hoped to protect from [word] of tourists.

7. Fenn reported the find on his website, stating only, “It was under a [word] of stars in the lush, forested vegetation of the Rocky Mountains and had not moved from the spot where I hid it more than 10 years ago.” 

8. Many searchers were outraged, demanding that Fenn reveal where the treasure had been hidden all these years. Without knowledge of the treasure’s location, they claimed, they could not get closure. Some even implied that something more sinister was afoot—that Fenn’s refusal to disclose who the finder was and where the treasure was hidden indicated that he had never hidden anything at all.

###A partial resolution

9. Things changed in the second half of 2020. Fenn died in September of that year, and then in December the finder of the treasure was hit with a lawsuit by a woman who claimed he had stolen her solution. At that time, the finder finally agreed to an interview, revealing himself to be Jack Stuef, a 32-year-old medical student who had solved the puzzle by carefully combing through each of Fenn’s interviews looking for clues and combining what he had learned from the interviews with the information in the poem. 

10. Despite finally knowing the finder’s identity, [word] of the hunt were still unsatisfied—what they really wanted was the location where the treasure had been hidden. However, as of 2023, Stuef remains silent on this issue, stating only that Fenn wished the location to remain unknown. So, for now, the mystery of the Fenn treasure will remain just that: a mystery.

questions.question_content: **Which TWO sections of the text best explain Fenn’s motivation for designing the treasure hunt?**

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
